facts.text: Matinecock Village traces its roots to the 17th‑century Native American Matinecock tribe that once spanned from Newtown to the Nissequogue River.
In 1643, settlers Robert Fordham and John Carman crossed the Long Island Sound to negotiate land with local tribes, founding the area that would become Matinecock.
The village’s name honors the Matinecock people, whose territory was later absorbed into the Town of Hempstead and Nassau County.
Friends Academy School opened in 1876, founded by Gideon Frost to serve the children of the local Quaker community.
Matinecock’s railroad history includes a terminus at Locust Valley until 1889, when the line extended to Oyster Bay, linking the village to broader Long Island trade.
The village’s first incorporated status dates to May 6, 1853, making it the earliest incorporated community in what is now Nassau County.
